title: CSS Fundamentals
description: Selectors, the box model, layout techniques, and responsive design principles.

sections.title: Selectors
sections.content:
CSS selectors are patterns used to select and style HTML elements. Mastering selectors is the foundation of writing efficient CSS.

Basic selectors

/* Element selector */
p { color: blue; }

/* Class selector */
.highlight { background: yellow; }

/* ID selector */
#header { font-size: 2rem; }

/* Universal selector */
* { box-sizing: border-box; }

Combinators

  • A B — Descendant (B anywhere inside A)
  • A > B — Direct child only
  • A + B — Adjacent sibling (B immediately after A)
  • A ~ B — General sibling (any B after A at the same level)

Pseudo-classes

a:hover { color: red; }
li:first-child { font-weight: bold; }
input:focus { outline: 2px solid blue; }
tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f0f0f0; }

sections.title: The Box Model
sections.content:
Every HTML element is a rectangular box. The CSS box model describes how the size of that box is calculated.

From inside to outside, the box has four layers:

  1. Content — The actual text, image, or child elements
  2. Padding — Space between content and border (transparent)
  3. Border — A line around the padding
  4. Margin — Space outside the border (transparent, collapses vertically)
.box {
  width: 200px;
  padding: 20px;
  border: 2px solid black;
  margin: 10px;
}

By default (box-sizing: content-box), width/height only apply to the content area. The total rendered width would be: 200 + 20×2 + 2×2 + 10×2 = 264px.

Use box-sizing: border-box to include padding and border in the width/height calculation. This is the modern standard and dramatically simplifies layout math.

sections.title: Flexbox
sections.content:
Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout method for arranging items in rows or columns. It excels at distributing space and aligning items.

.container {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;  /* main axis */
  align-items: center;      /* cross axis */
  gap: 1rem;
}

Key container properties

  • flex-direction — row (default), column, row-reverse, column-reverse
  • flex-wrap — nowrap (default), wrap
  • justify-content — Main axis alignment: flex-start, center, space-between, space-around, space-evenly
  • align-items — Cross axis alignment: stretch (default), center, flex-start, flex-end

Key item properties

.item {
  flex: 1;          /* shorthand: grow shrink basis */
  flex-grow: 1;     /* how much to grow */
  flex-shrink: 0;   /* don't shrink */
  flex-basis: 200px; /* ideal starting size */
  align-self: center; /* override container's align-items */
}

sections.title: Responsive Design
sections.content:
Responsive design ensures your layout works across all screen sizes. The key tools are media queries, relative units, and fluid layouts.

Media queries

/* Mobile-first approach */
.grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr;
}

@media (min-width: 768px) {
  .grid {
    grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
  }
}

@media (min-width: 1024px) {
  .grid {
    grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
  }
}

Relative units

  • rem — Relative to root font size (usually 16px)
  • em — Relative to parent element's font size
  • vw / vh — 1% of viewport width / height
  • % — Relative to parent element's dimension
The viewport meta tag is essential: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">. Without it, mobile browsers render at desktop width and scale down.

questions.text: Which selector has the highest specificity?
questions.explanation: Specificity from lowest to highest: universal (*) → element (p) → class (.box) → ID (#header). ID selectors have a specificity weight of 1-0-0, class selectors 0-1-0, and element selectors 0-0-1.

questions.text: With box-sizing: border-box, padding and border are included in the element's width and height.
questions.explanation: That is exactly what border-box does. If you set width: 200px on an element with border-box, the total rendered width will be 200px regardless of padding and border values. The content area shrinks to accommodate them.

questions.text: What does 'justify-content' control in a flex container?
questions.explanation: justify-content distributes space along the main axis (horizontal by default). For cross-axis alignment, you'd use align-items or align-content.

questions.text: The 'rem' unit is relative to the parent element's font size.
questions.explanation: rem stands for root em and is relative to the root element's (<html>) font size — not the parent's. The em unit is the one relative to the parent element's font size.

questions.text: Which combinator selects only direct children?
questions.explanation: The > combinator selects only direct (immediate) children. The space combinator selects all descendants, + selects adjacent siblings, and ~ selects general siblings.

questions.text: In the box model, which layer is between padding and margin?
questions.explanation: The box model layers from inside out are: content → padding → border → margin. The outline sits outside the margin and does not affect layout.
